# Transcrição: 16-Doing Time

- **Fonte:** 16-Doing Time
- **Duração:** 07:49
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:04]

Keep it going for your first story teller! All kinds of noise from Matthew Dix! Thank you. It's early on a Saturday morning. My daughter Clara, who's four at the time, and I are the only two awake in the house. She's in the back room. She's playing with her Playmobile people. She's making up stories. I hear Playmobile daughter ask Playmobile Daddy for a pony. And Playmobile Daddy says, sure, how about two? I'm always, like, terrified with the responses that Playmobile Daddy gives Playmobile daughter. They're, like, ridiculously generous at all times. I'm sitting in the dining room and I'm writing stories too. I'm working on my next novel. When I feel a tap on my shoulder, it scares the shit out of me. I turn and it's Clara. She's smiling at me. And then she opens her mouth. And the words that come out of her mouth astound me. And they throw me into this new world. It's not the fictional world I was just in, but it's a world of truth that happened long ago. When I was a boy, I was not a well-behaved person. It was no malice in my heart. I was simply born with a rejection of boundaries. I just couldn't stand to be told what to do and when to do it. I got in trouble for the first time in kindergarten. Week two, it was indoor recess. And I've gathered all the blocks somehow in the whole classroom. And I'm building this tower, this like majestic structure, like rising from the linoleum. And the bell rings. And it's the end of recess, but I still have blocks left and a vision for like what this needs to be. And so I just keep building. And Mrs. Dubois, my kindergarten teacher, says, Matt, you got to put the blocks away. And I say, yeah, I know. Just give me a minute. And then she's standing over me, reminding me I need to put the blocks away. And I'm like, I'm just, I'm building here. And so this kind and beautiful woman slides her foot underneath my tower. And it topples in front of me. Like I can't believe it. I don't remember what the actual words were, but they were the five year old version of, who the fuck do you think you are, woman? And it's whatever it is. It's like angry enough that it gets her angry. And she points to the corner and says, go. And I do for the first time, I go to the corner. I stand under the American flag right next to the pencil sharpener. And I am there 10 seconds when I have like this realization that changes my life forever. I realize this punishment has no teeth. I'm just standing in a corner. I can do this all day. It's the moment when I realize truly at five years old that punishment is not a bad thing. It's just a trade. I trade time for choice. I do whatever the fuck I want. And I just give time. And that is what happens. I just become an unmanageable person because all I do is go forth and balance that equation. Time for choice. And the punishments change.

## [03:04 - 06:09]

Like they start taking away my recess and then in high school I get after school detentions and suspensions. But I am always able to balance that equation. I'm always able to find something that I want to do that is equal to whatever the hell they can do to me. Until 10th grade when Mr. Powers, the new vice principal, arrives. He introduces something new to that time choice equation. And it is lectures. In addition to giving me time, he needs to tell me for 30 minutes what a waste I am and what a failure I am. And I am unable to find the balance in that equation. There is nothing I can find that can equal the lectures that I have to endure from this man. Until one day, 10th grade biology, Mrs. Murphy's class, she's got an eye wash in the classroom. It's this big green bucket with a handle that says push an emergency. Like my life is a constant state of emergency. If someone should push that handle, it needs to be me. And I stare at this bucket all year and just try to figure out like, how does it work? And so one day Mrs. Murphy is absent and there's a substitute teacher in school and she is foolish enough to go forth with the experiment we had planned that day. So like chaos erupts as Bunsen burners and test tubes and this camouflage for me. And so I grab that eye wash and I bring it to the back of the room. And at last I push in case of emergency. I can't believe what happens. Like more water comes forth from this eye wash than could physically be in the eye wash. It is like a clown car of water that comes flying out. It's like, it's a Harry Potter bucket of endless water and it is everywhere. I am instantly soaked and everyone around me is soaked. It's like a fountain in the back of the classroom. It's the best thing that happens to me all year long. But at the same time the substitute teacher does not go unnoticed. And so she points the door and she says, you office now. And I go, I'm going to go get the lecture and the time. And I know as I'm going down there that she's going to do this thing all teachers do. They call in a preemptive strike. They call Mr. Powers ahead of time so they can get like their argument in before I can ever say anything. And I know she's doing it. And as I'm walking down there just something snaps. I just decide I'm not doing the lecture. I'm not doing it anymore. And so I walk into the office and instead of sitting at the little bench and wait my turn. I just walk through that little flapping door and right towards his office. And the secretary see me. I am drenched. Like I am not invisible in any way. And they just let me go right into his office. I push the door open and he looks up and he knows why I'm here. And he points at the chair. I always sit in and I say, no. I say, listen, I know what you're going to say. And you know what I'm going to say. So why don't you just save us some time and give me my time and I will leave. And there is this glorious second in my life where Mr. Powers does not know what to say. He is stuck for one second and it is joyous. And then he regains his footing and he says, fine, but it's three days of in school suspension.

## [06:09 - 07:49]

And that has some bite. But as I leave the office, like something has fundamentally changed inside me. From that point on, I just start saying whatever the hell I want to whoever I want. And if you know me today, that is the primary problem in my life at all times. That I have lost complete control of my mouth. It is why I'm standing here today speaking because I have just spent my life saying what I want to whoever I want directly from the heart. And so now I am 25 years later in that dining room and my daughter, Clara, is tapping me on the shoulder. And when I turn, for no reason that I can discern, for no reason that I can figure out has popped into her head. She says to me, Daddy, I can say whatever I want and no one can ever do anything about it. And I like grab the dining room table for strength. I cannot believe what my daughter has just said to me. And at that moment, I am so filled with pride. My daughter who looks exactly like my wife, I realize inside she is exactly like me. But I also pray to God that there is enough of my wife in her heart as well. Enough of her to silence her at moments when silence is required. Because I know what happens when you never stop your mouth and you say whatever you want whenever you want. And so I stare at my daughter, part Alisha, part me, and secretly hoping that she's a little more my wife than she is me. Thank you.
